compliance_requirements.requirement_summary: Ansvar summary: ISO 27036-1 establishes the framework for information security in supplier relationships, defining key concepts including acquirer, supplier, ICT product/service, and supply chain. It requires organisations to treat supplier relationships as a first-class information security concern and to implement governance structures for supplier risk management.

Ansvar summary: ISO 27036-2 specifies requirements for supplier relationship information security, including: defining information security requirements in procurement processes, incorporating security requirements into supplier agreements, monitoring and reviewing supplier service delivery and information security, and managing changes to supplier services. Contracts must address security expectations, monitoring rights, incident handling, and termination/transition obligations.

Ansvar summary: Supplier agreements shall include: description of information to be provided/accessed, classification of information, legal and regulatory requirements, obligation to implement controls, incident reporting procedures, right to audit, sub-supplier management requirements, and information handling/return/destruction on termination.

Ansvar summary: ISO 27036-3 provides guidelines for ICT supply chain security, addressing risks specific to ICT products and services including: malicious functionality, counterfeit components, vulnerabilities in third-party software, and ICT supply chain attack vectors. Contracts for ICT products/services should address provenance verification, integrity assurance, vulnerability disclosure, and secure development practices.

Ansvar summary: Acquirers should require suppliers to provide evidence of the provenance and integrity of ICT products and services, including software bills of materials (SBOM), secure development lifecycle evidence, and tamper-evidence mechanisms. Contracts should specify provenance documentation requirements and rights to verify integrity.

Ansvar summary: ISO 27036-4 provides guidelines for information security of cloud services, addressing: shared responsibility models, data location and jurisdiction, portability and interoperability, incident management, and exit planning. Cloud service contracts must clearly define the shared responsibility model, data residency requirements, and exit/migration provisions.

Ansvar summary: Cloud service agreements must include exit planning provisions addressing: data export in standard formats, transition assistance period, data deletion confirmation, and provisions for continuity during migration. Exit plans should be tested periodically.

Ansvar summary: Cloud service agreements must clearly delineate security responsibilities between the cloud service customer and cloud service provider for each cloud deployment and service model (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS). The shared responsibility matrix should be documented, regularly reviewed, and updated when services change.

Ansvar summary: Controllers shall determine and document the controller-processor relationship obligations in a contract. The contract shall address: processing only on documented instructions, confidentiality of personnel, security measures, sub-processor engagement, data subject rights assistance, deletion/return of data, audit support, and notification of instruction infringement. Aligns with GDPR Art 28(3).

Ansvar summary: Processors shall process PII only in accordance with the documented instructions of the controller (or the customer in the case of multi-tier processing). The processor shall not process PII for any purpose other than as instructed and shall promptly inform the controller if an instruction appears to infringe applicable legislation.

Ansvar summary: Processors shall not engage sub-processors without the prior written authorisation (general or specific) of the controller. Where general authorisation is given, the processor shall inform the controller of changes to sub-processors and allow the controller to object. Sub-processor contracts must impose equivalent obligations.

Ansvar summary: The organisation shall identify and document the basis for cross-border transfers of PII. Where transfers rely on contractual clauses, the organisation shall ensure the clauses provide an adequate level of protection as determined by applicable legislation and that the clauses address supplementary measures where required.

Ansvar summary: The organisation shall implement privacy by design and privacy by default. Contracts with processors shall require implementation of appropriate technical and organisational measures to support the controller's privacy-by-design obligations, including data minimisation, purpose limitation, and privacy-enhancing technologies.

Ansvar summary: The entity assesses and manages risks associated with vendors and business partners. The vendor management control requires documented vendor selection and assessment, risk-based categorisation, contractual security obligations, ongoing monitoring and periodic re-assessment, and incident response coordination with vendors.

Ansvar summary: Where a service organisation uses subservice organisations (sub-processors), the SOC 2 report must identify controls that the subservice organisation is expected to implement (CSOCs). Contracts with sub-processors must specify the expected CSOCs and require evidence of their operation, typically through the sub-processor's own SOC 2 report.

Ansvar summary: SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria cover five categories: Security (CC), Availability (A), Processing Integrity (PI), Confidentiality (C), and Privacy (P). Vendor contracts must address the applicable trust services criteria and specify the vendor's obligations for each included criterion, with requirements for annual assessment and evidence of control effectiveness.

Ansvar summary: SOC 2 reports cover a specified review period (typically 12 months for Type II). Contracts should require vendors to undergo annual SOC 2 Type II examinations, provide copies of SOC 2 reports and bridge letters as applicable, and notify the entity of any qualified opinions, significant exceptions, or material changes to the control environment.

Ansvar summary: The entity restricts logical and physical access to information assets. Contracts with vendors who have logical or physical access to systems must specify access controls, authentication requirements, access provisioning and deprovisioning procedures, and restrictions on vendor access scope and duration.

Ansvar summary: ISO 22301 requires organisations to determine and implement business continuity strategies for their prioritised activities, including those dependent on suppliers. Contracts with critical suppliers must include: business continuity requirements, recovery time objectives (RTOs), recovery point objectives (RPOs), testing participation, alternative supply arrangements, and communication procedures during disruption.

Ansvar summary: Where an organisation is dependent on suppliers for the delivery of prioritised products and services, the organisation shall assess the risk of supplier disruption and implement measures to reduce dependency risk. Contracts shall address: alternative supplier arrangements, key person dependencies, supplier financial stability monitoring, and coordinated recovery testing.
